unit.name: Backpropagation on computation graphs
unit.description: Recasts neural networks as computation graphs and derives backward-pass rules from local gradients and branch structure.
unit.summary: Backpropagation is distilled to two ideas: apply the chain rule and store intermediate results so repeated work is avoided [ts=2711s][ts=2733s]. The network is redrawn as a computation graph whose forward pass computes values node by node—for example x to Wx to z to h to s [ts=2776s][ts=2811s]. The backward pass starts from ds/ds = 1 and sends gradients backward by multiplying each node's upstream gradient by its local gradient [ts=2867s][ts=2947s]. The instructor then uses a toy graph f(x,y,z) = (x + y) * max(y,z) with x = 1, y = 2, z = 0 to show how forward values are computed [ts=3044s][ts=3083s], how local derivatives are assigned for plus, max, and multiply [ts=3125s][ts=3191s], and why branch outputs require summing incoming gradient contributions at shared variables [ts=3379s][ts=3412s].

key_points.text: Backpropagation is just the chain rule plus caching of intermediate results.
A neural network can be represented as a computation graph whose source nodes are inputs and whose interior nodes are operations.
The backward pass starts from ds/ds = 1 and computes downstream gradients as upstream gradient times local gradient.
For nodes with multiple inputs, a separate local gradient is taken with respect to each input.
In the toy graph example, the forward pass evaluates f(x,y,z) = (x + y) * max(y,z) to 6 when x=1, y=2, z=0.
At graph branches, gradient contributions from different paths are summed at the shared variable.

unit_summary_for_generation: Backpropagation is distilled to two ideas: apply the chain rule and store intermediate results so repeated work is avoided [ts=2711s][ts=2733s]. The network is redrawn as a computation graph whose forward pass computes values node by node—for example x to Wx to z to h to s [ts=2776s][ts=2811s]. The backward pass starts from ds/ds = 1 and sends gradients backward by multiplying each node's upstream gradient by its local gradient [ts=2867s][ts=2947s]. The instructor then uses a toy graph f(x,y,z) = (x + y) * max(y,z) with x = 1, y = 2, z = 0 to show how forward values are computed [ts=3044s][ts=3083s], how local derivatives are assigned for plus, max, and multiply [ts=3125s][ts=3191s], and why branch outputs require summing incoming gradient contributions at shared variables [ts=3379s][ts=3412s].

kp_catalog.name: Upstream gradient reuse and outer-product parameter gradients
kp_catalog.description: Shared upstream error signals should be reused across parameter derivatives, and weight gradients often combine the upstream signal with the local input in an outer-product form.
kp_catalog.importance_rationale: Important because upstream gradient reuse and outer-product parameter gradients clarifies shared upstream error signals should be reused across parameter derivatives, and weight gradients often combine the upstream signal with the local input in an outer-product form, sharpening later modeling, analysis, or evaluation choices.

kp_catalog.name: Computational graphs and automatic differentiation
kp_catalog.description: General reverse-mode autodiff runs forward in topological order on a DAG and backward in reverse topological order, which is how modern frameworks implement backpropagation. A neural network can be understood as a directed acyclic graph of operations whose forward pass computes values and whose backward pass computes gradients.
kp_catalog.importance_rationale: Important because computational graphs and automatic differentiation formalizes how general reverse-mode autodiff runs forward in topological order on a DAG and backward in reverse topological order, which is how modern frameworks implement backpropagation, which later units use as core machinery rather than background context while roles split between gateway and supporting and importance labels split between critical and high.

kp_catalog.name: Gradient routing and accumulation
kp_catalog.description: Different node types route gradients differently: addition copies the upstream gradient, max routes it to the argmax input, multiplication swaps forward coefficients, and branches require summing contributions.
kp_catalog.importance_rationale: Important because gradient routing and accumulation clarifies different node types route gradients differently: addition copies the upstream gradient, max routes it to the argmax input, multiplication swaps forward coefficients, and branches require summing contributions, sharpening later modeling, analysis, or evaluation choices.
